How to use this map−
What this shows. Legal defense and prisoner support means the people and organisations that stand between a person and the machinery of punishment — public defenders and legal aid, bail and bond funds, innocence and wrongful-conviction projects, death-penalty and extreme-sentence defense, prisoner and family support, prison-conditions monitoring, prisoners’ rights litigation, reentry services, and the data and watchdogs that keep the system visible. This map plots those organisations for every country — and for every state or province where legal aid is administered locally — alongside the physical infrastructure of criminal justice worldwide: prisons, courthouses, police stations and more.

How complete is it? Two honest measures — raw (share of all units on Earth at that tier, most of which have no discoverable organisation) and realistic (share of units that plausibly have a findable, verifiable provider). These figures cover this map’s scope — defense, detention and prisoner support; they do not measure civil, family or immigration law except where it meets criminal defense. Every URL is verified; nothing is invented.
LevelOn maprawrealistic
International28~75%~90%
National1376 / 174 countries~31%~70%
State / province65 / 58 regions—~40%
County / district68 / 62 counties——
Municipal5 / 5 cities——
Facilities plotted176,192 pointsevery building, worldwide
The organisation index has five possible tiers — international, national, state/province, county/district and municipal. State/province is populated in the seven federal systems where legal aid is run locally (USA, Australia, Canada, UK, South Africa, India, Brazil); the county and municipal tiers are scaffolded but not yet filled — a blank unit means “no separate source added yet,” not “unchecked,” and below the state line a national or state provider already covers the ground. The facility layer is different — it plots individual buildings down to the street, 176,192 of them, but those are physical locations, not clickable accountability sources. Figures below the international line are estimates (±15 pts). The map runs UK-heavy at the national tier because its voluntary sector is unusually well-indexed — that reflects data availability, not the distribution of need.

Navigate. Click a country to zoom in — its regions load and any holding sources light up. Click a lit region for its public defenders, legal-aid offices, innocence projects and other providers, and to drill into its sub-divisions. Click empty space or zoom out to step back up.

Can’t find a resource where you are? Coverage below the national level is still filling in, and many towns, districts and even whole regions have no separate entry yet — a blank area means “nothing mapped here yet,” not “nothing exists.” If nothing local comes up for your area, open the nearest entry that does cover your region — a national or state legal-aid body, a public-defender service, a bar or law-society referral line, a prisoners’-rights or prisoner-and-family support organisation, or a human-rights or ombudsman office with a mandate over your area — and contact them directly. These organisations routinely refer people onward to local lawyers, clinics, bail funds and support groups that never made it onto this map, and higher-level bodies almost always know who works on the ground beneath them. Start with whoever covers your region and ask them who covers your town.

Why some places show only a distant watchdog. In a few of the most closed states, the only entry you’ll find is an exile-based documentation group rather than a local clinic or defense lawyer — and where that is so, the government has banned, dissolved or forced into exile the independent legal-aid and prisoner-support organisations that would otherwise appear here. Eritrea, for instance, permits no independent NGOs at all; North Korea has no independent civil society; and Turkmenistan’s rights groups operate only from abroad. In those specific cases the distant entry is still a worthwhile first contact — it often knows who, if anyone, can help on the ground. This explanation applies only where it is actually documented; a sparse map anywhere else usually just means coverage is still being built, not that anyone was silenced. And absence is never proof. This map is not complete: it misses organisations, and new ones open (and closed ones reopen) all the time. Even under a repressive government there may be quiet or informal help this map simply hasn’t found — so treat an empty map as a reason to keep looking, not a verdict that no one is there.

The coloured dots are physical buildings, not the organisations above — the places where criminal justice happens. Zoom in to see them: ● prisons & jails, ● courthouses, ● public defenders, ● legal aid, ● police, ● immigration detention, ● probation & parole, ● juvenile facilities. Click a dot for its name & link; toggle types off in the legend (bottom-right) to browse regions freely.

Two ways to filter. 1· Lens pills (right) — each lens is a kind of help, grouped by stage: Accused & awaiting trial (Defense & Counsel, Pretrial & Bail, Innocence & Appeals), Inside (Prison Conditions, Death Penalty & Extreme Sentences, Prisoner & Family Support), and Rights, release & evidence (Prisoners’ Rights & Litigation, Human & Indigenous Rights, Reentry & Restoration, Data & Watchdogs). Selecting one dims every country and region that lacks a source of that type, so coverage and gaps show at a glance. Below the pills, narrow further by source type, trust, media and voice. 2· The index (below) — the full directory of every organisation on the map; group & sort with the two drop-downs and search by name.
🌐 Global Wire
The justice climate — a standing overview, with a live wire beneath drawn from a rotating selection of the world’s leading accountability news sources (not all of them).
Before ~1700. Punishment was mostly swift and public — fines, exile, mutilation, death. Prisons held people awaiting judgment rather than serving it. Defense was whatever a person could argue alone, and mercy came from a ruler’s discretion, not a right. Across empires from Rome to China, sanctuary, ransom and clemency were the closest thing to appeal.

1700s–1800s. Imprisonment itself became the punishment, and with it the penitentiary — reformers promised rehabilitation through isolation and labour; what often followed was silence, sickness and death. The same era wrote the first real defenses: the right to counsel, habeas corpus, rules of evidence, the presumption of innocence. Yet the cell and the colony grew together, and convict transportation and forced labour carried punishment across the world.

1800s–1945. Prison systems industrialised and spread through empire, and the law’s protections stopped at the colonial line. Penal colonies, forced-labour camps and mass political detention defined the age; states learned that the cell could hold not just the criminal but the dissenter. Prisoner aid societies and the first prison-reform movements began keeping records the state would not.

1945–2000s. The postwar order named the rights that detention had denied: no torture, fair trial, counsel for those who cannot pay. The Standard Minimum Rules, the Convention against Torture and its inspection regime, regional human-rights courts, national legal-aid systems and public-defender offices all took shape. Prisoners’ rights entered the courtroom, and litigation began forcing prisons open to scrutiny.

Today (2010s–now). Two currents run against each other. One is protection’s advance: independent monitors with a right of entry, DNA evidence overturning convictions, bail funds and decarceration campaigns, a global retreat from the death penalty, and reporting from inside that no wall fully contains. The other is confinement’s: record prison populations, pretrial detention holding the presumed innocent for years, immigration detention beyond ordinary law, and legal aid starved while caseloads climb. The meter reads contest, not resolution — more rights exist on paper than ever, and more people are held than ever.

Veins to watch. Watch the defense: legal-aid and public-defender budgets starved while caseloads climb past any honest capacity (a lawyer with four hundred felony files), eligibility narrowed until the poor no longer qualify, and the right to counsel arriving too late to matter (no lawyer at the first bail hearing). Watch the wait: pretrial detention that punishes before any verdict — cash bail set beyond reach, remand stretching into years, and backlogs engineered rather than fixed (the presumed innocent held longer than the sentence would be). Watch the walls: monitors denied entry or quietly defunded, inspection reports unpublished, deaths in custody uninvestigated, solitary confinement renamed rather than ended, and prison healthcare thinned until illness becomes a sentence of its own. Watch the record: evidence lost, forensics unreviewed, disclosure withheld, and post-conviction doors closed by deadline rather than by merit (a claim of innocence barred as filed too late). Watch the sharpest edge: executions resumed after long pause, death rows built where the crime rate did not move, life-without-parole handed to children, and clemency machinery left to rust. Watch who is held apart: immigration detention placed beyond ordinary courts, and women, juveniles, disabled and foreign prisoners routed into the least-visible facilities (detention where no monitor has standing to enter). Watch the ties outside: visits and phone calls priced or cancelled, mail digitised and read, books refused at the gate, and families made to fund the state’s custody of their own. Watch the door out: parole boards politicised, release delayed for want of housing, records that bar work and the vote for life, and reentry funded as an afterthought (a sentence that never actually ends). And watch the watchers: the ombudsmen, inspectorates, monitoring bodies and bar associations meant to keep all of this honest — captured, defunded, or filled with the very officials they exist to check. Any one of these can carry an innocent explanation.
